title: LEVENTHORPE v THE CHIEF CONSTABLE
text:
Background




1. On the 10th January 2006 Adrian Edward Digby Leventhorpe (the Appellant) having pleaded guilty to a charge of driving having consumed so much alcohol that the proportion of his breath exceeded the prescribed limit was sentenced to 42 days custody, licence endorsed and disqualified from driving for 7 years. He was also ordered to retake his test at the end of the period of disqualification. 




2. On the 11th January 2006 the Appellant lodged his appeal against sentence. The grounds of the appeal were stated to be as follows 芒聙聹The learned High Bailiff failed to give adequate weight to the Psychiatric Report which detailed the Appellant芒聙聶s bi polar disorder when considering a custodial sentence. The Appellant will submit that the High Bailiff should have ordered the custodial sentence to be suspended芒聙聺. 




Facts




3. The facts in relation to this matter were as follows. On Tuesday the 22nd November 2005 at about 11 p.m. acting on information received PC 211 Gorry and PC 131 Footer attended the area of Central Promenade, Douglas. At that time they witnessed a green Daihatsu registration EMN 406T drive along the promenade towards Queens Promenade, Douglas. This vehicle was caused to stop and the driver, the Appellant, was subsequently spoken to. PC Gorry stated that he could smell intoxicants from the Appellant芒聙聶s breath, his eyes were glazed and his speech was slurred. A roadside breath test was conducted which resulted in a positive reading. The Appellant was subsequently arrested and cautioned to which he made no reply. At Police Headquarters the Appellant gave two samples of breath on the lion intoxilyzer. The lowest reading being 146ugs. At 11.30 a.m. on Wednesday 23rd November 2005 at Police Headquarters the Appellant was interviewed on tape under caution. He stated that he had been drinking vodka and beer since midday and used the vehicle to drive to an off licence to purchase more drink. At 12.54 p.m. on Wednesday 23rd November 2005 at Police Headquarters the Appellant was charged with the offences for which he appeared before the High Bailiff. Under caution he made no reply. 




4. We also note the previous conviction in October 1996 for driving a motor car with excess alcohol. Some time ago now but a serious aggravating factor. 




5. We have full regard to the points raised in mitigation by Mr O芒聙聶Neill in the court below and to the aggravating factors. 




Sentencing Remarks of the High Bailiff




6. We also have full regard to the sentencing remarks of His Worship the High Bailiff. 




7. We refer to the following extract from such remarks. The extract appears on page 5 of the transcript: 




芒聙聹I芒聙聶ve had to have regard as to whether prison will be so detrimental to you that it would be oppressive for me to send you there, because the views of the courts are well known, this is the second time you芒聙聶ve been before the Court, once in England now before me, for an offence of this nature. Custody is the obvious thing the Court has in mind. I have to decide whether having looked at the reports, prison would be so detrimental as I say to be oppressive to you. Dr Haq makes no such observation. As regard to the history of this matter and to your previous history I think and I regret to say this, but I would be failing my duty if I weren芒聙聶t to mark this offence with a custodial sentence. It芒聙聶s one of the more serious matters that come before the Court. As I say you have a previous conviction and you were four times the limit. I芒聙聶m sure that the shame and disgrace will have an effect on you, and it may indeed make you in future behave and try and co-operate. But at the moment I芒聙聶m sorry that custody is the only sentence I think芒聙聶s appropriate and I don芒聙聶t pass the sentence because I want to, it芒聙聶s because I feel I have no alternative. I will reduce the normal sentence, a normal sentence would have been round about 60 days plus for this sort of offence Mr Leventhorpe. You will go to prison for 42 days, licence is endorsed. I was minded to say well you should be disqualified or, whether it should be open ended, but I芒聙聶m going to disqualify you in the circumstances for 7 years, you芒聙聶ll take your test again at the end of the period and your disqualification also applies in the United Kingdom. Thank you.芒聙聺




8. Those were the closing words of the High Bailiff芒聙聶s sentencing remarks in respect of this case. 




Reports




9. We have considered all the documents, evidence and submissions placed before the court including the psychiatric report of Dr Haq dated the 3rd January 2006 and the social enquiry report completed on the 22nd December 2005. 




10. We note from the social enquiry report at paragraph 2.4 that the Appellant accepts responsibility for his behaviour. He also accepts that he has to take the consequences for his actions. 




11. We have also had regard to the psychiatric report of Dr O芒聙聶Sullivan dated the 12th day of January 2006. We gave leave to the Appellant to adduce this further evidence on appeal. We have considered that report very carefully and we note in particular the reference that the doctor is concerned that a further period in prison will cause the Appellant to regress and to have a severe exacerbation in his mood disorder. The Respondent submits that such statement lacks further particularity. 




Submissions




12. Mr O芒聙聶Neill for the Appellant accepts that the High Bailiff was correct to impose a custodial sentence in all the circumstances of this case. 




13. Mr O芒聙聶Neill submits that in considering whether to suspend the sentence the High Bailiff had regard to the correct provisions but failed to give sufficient weight to the psychiatric evidence before the court in relation to the bi polar disorder and its relevance to the actual offence and its affect on the Appellant whilst in custody. 




14. Mr O芒聙聶Neill also refers in detail to the report of Dr O芒聙聶Sullivan. Mr O芒聙聶Neill submits that the High Bailiff should have suspended the sentence or imposed a suspended sentence supervision order. 




15. Mr O芒聙聶Neill has raised every conceivable point on the Appellant芒聙聶s behalf. 




16. Miss Braidwood for the Respondent submits that the sentence imposed by the High Bailiff on the information available to him was not excessive and was entirely appropriate and right in principle. 




Legal Principles




17. The relevant legal principles are well settled. This court would not normally interfere with a sentence imposed by a court below unless that sentence was shown to be manifestly excessive or wrong in principle (Perryv Clague 1961-1971 MLR 162). We note also the sentencing guidelines in Cooper v Oake 1987-89 MLR 390 and Thompson v Oake 1993-95 MLR 11. 




18. There is no suggestion that the High Bailiff did not have regard to the relevant sentencing guidelines. 




19. We have regard to the judgment of this court in Jameson delivered on the 29th April 2005 and the position in respect of suspended sentences. The circumstances and the medical evidence in Jameson were very different to the circumstances and medical evidence in the case before us this afternoon. 




Determination of the Appeal




20. In the case we are presently dealing with it is plain that the High Bailiff took full account of the condition the Appellant suffers from. There are references to this in the exchanges with counsel and in the High Bailiff芒聙聶s sentencing remarks. 




21. Moreover it is plain that the High Bailiff, albeit without the benefit of the report from Dr O芒聙聶Sullivan of the 12th January 2006, had regard to the affect a custodial sentence would have on the Appellant in particular taking into account the medical condition of the Appellant. 




22. There were serious aggravating factors in this case. The Appellant had a previous conviction for a similar offence albeit some considerable time ago. Moreover we have regard to the very high reading of alcohol. The High Bailiff referred to the history of the lack of co-operation with respect to treatment. The Appellant made a conscious decision to drive motivated by the fact that he had run out of alcohol and wanted more. The High Bailiff took into account the medical condition of the Appellant. That is clear from his sentencing remarks and from the exchanges with counsel. It is for the prison authorities to deal with any medical issues in respect of those in custody. 




23. The High Bailiff took into account the Appellant芒聙聶s medical condition and imposed a much reduced period of custody. 




24. The sentence imposed by the High Bailiff cannot be faulted. There are no grounds which would justify this court interfering with the sentence imposed by the High Bailiff. It was not manifestly excessive and it was not wrong in principle. It follows for those reasons that we dismiss this appeal.
court: HIGH COURT OF JUSTICE OF THE ISLE OF MAN
party_two: MICHAEL CULVERHOUSE - THE CHIEF CONSTABLE OF THE ISLE OF MAN
